topic: Anthropic in talks with Samsung to manufacture custom AI chip
summary: Anthropic is in talks with Samsung to potentially collaborate on a custom AI chip, while OpenAI is accelerating its efforts to develop its own AI silicon, with plans to finalize its first in-house chip design this year and partner with South Korean chip giants.

sections.paragraph: Anthropic, a leading artificial intelligence startup, has reportedly been in talks with Samsung for a potential collaboration on developing a custom AI chip, although details about the proposed processor remain unclear. The company has also recently raised $65 billion in a Series H funding round, valuing it at $965 billion and surpassing OpenAI as the most valuable AI startup. This significant funding round, led by prominent investors, has nearly tripled Anthropic's previous valuation of $380 billion. The increased valuation widens the competitive gap between Anthropic and OpenAI, particularly as both companies consider stock market listings, with OpenAI planning to file for an initial public offering and Anthropic potentially following suit. The funding and potential partnerships, such as the one with Samsung, underscore Anthropic's growing influence and ambitions in the AI sector. The company's infrastructure approach currently relies on chips supplied by Google and Amazon, but the exploration of custom AI chip development suggests a potential shift in its strategy.

How Anthropic became world's most valuable AI startup, surpassing OpenAI with $965 billion valuation | Company Business News

sections.paragraph: OpenAI is accelerating its efforts to develop its own artificial intelligence (AI) silicon, aiming to reduce dependence on Nvidia, with plans to finalize the design of its first in-house chip in the coming months. The company intends to send its design for fabrication to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC), marking a significant milestone in its ambition to mass-produce its AI chips by 2026. This move is reportedly driven by rising costs and dependence on a single supplier, a concern also shared by other major customers such as Microsoft and Meta. OpenAI's in-house AI chip will initially be deployed on a limited scale, primarily for running AI models, and is capable of both training and running AI models. The process of submitting a chip design for production, known as 'taping out', can cost tens of millions of dollars and take approximately six months to complete, with no guarantee that the first iteration will function as intended. If successful, this could potentially disrupt Nvidia's market share, which currently stands at around 80%.

OpenAI set to finalize first custom chip design this year

sections.paragraph: OpenAI has formed partnerships with South Korean tech giants Samsung and SK to support its Stargate project, a $500 billion initiative focused on building artificial intelligence infrastructure. The partnerships, announced after a meeting between OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and South Korean President Lee Jae Myung, aim to provide advanced chips and other solutions for the project. Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix, the world's two largest memory chip makers, will accelerate production to meet OpenAI's increasing memory demands for Stargate. The South Korean government views the partnerships as a significant opportunity for the country's semiconductor industry to strengthen its role in AI and create more jobs. The collaboration highlights the growing importance of international partnerships in the development of AI infrastructure. The Stargate project's massive scale and investment underscore the geopolitical significance of AI development and the competition for technological leadership.

sources.title: OpenAI announces partnerships with South Korean chip giants over Stargate project | AP News

sections.paragraph: The Indian smartphone market is experiencing significant economic shifts, with companies like Google and Apple prioritizing artificial intelligence, leading to increased demand for AI chips and higher component costs. As a result, smartphone prices are no longer dropping predictably and are instead launching at higher prices. The production-linked incentive (PLI) scheme has also impacted the market, with companies like Apple expanding production in India and taking advantage of the lack of tariffs on Indian smartphone exports to the US. However, the abolition of tariffs on Chinese electronics exports could hurt India's competitiveness due to China's well-developed supply chains and advanced manufacturing. Meanwhile, companies like Vivo are forming partnerships with Indian entities, such as Dixon, to comply with government regulations and scrutiny. The market is also seeing increased competition, with mid-tier rivals gaining ground and companies seeking new business to drive growth in a market facing stagnant volumes.
